Page 1

Bearing 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club | March - April, 1991

Coming Next : Lake Tradition

With a touch of spring in the air, we return to Lake Tradition Park. Lake Tradition lies on the first ridge to the east of Issaquah, and offers lots of contours and some marshes, ponds and a lake. Although there is very little open forest, most of the park is light green and runnable.

To reach the Park, take I-90 to the High Point Exit (Exit 20), several miles past Issaquah. Park along the exit road. Starts will be a short walk in from the parking area. Meet Director is Debbie Newell; courses by Glen Gordon. If you would like to help with registration/starts/finishes, please call Debbie at 488-7471. For latest meet information, call the Hotline: 783-3866.

[Image: Map of Lake Tradition area]

ROGAINES

COC is planning a 6 hr ROGAINE in the Tumac Mt area this August. Volunteer efforts gladly accepted. An 8 hr ROGAINE is planned near Tuscon, AZ on April 6, and RMOC is planning a 24 hr Colorado ROGAINE over Labor Day weekend. Bruce McAlister has details

> ATTENTION PLEASE
>
> Enclosed with this issue of Bearing 315 is a COC Questionnaire prepared by Rick Hood. A Club like ours depends upon knowing what YOU want. Please take the time to fill out and return the questionnaire - by mail (it takes 29c), or at any Club function. Family members please make copies and fill out individually. Extra copies (and stamps) available at club functions.

COC OFFER TO HOST '92 USOF CONVENTION

The COC Board has voted to notify USOF that COC will offer to host the 1992 USOF Convention. The proposed convention site is the UW Campus in early to mid July, after the 4th, but still well before the O-Ringen.

The Convention, typically Tuesday through Friday would be followed by a 2-day A meet. Early thinking has the A meet at Ros-Elum/Cle Elum Ridge. The convention would include various social and fun events; a canoe-o, night-o, and dinner at Blake Island. The Convention would be preceded by a Junior Training Camp.

Attendance at the Convention has typically been 75/100, with substantially more at the A meet. We want this to be a class-A event from the first welcoming to the final award presentation. This means a lot of work by a lot of people. All offers of help and ideas would be deeply appreciated. Committees are just being organized. Please call Bruce McAlister (821-6862), or leave a message on the Hot-Line if you are interested in helping.

WIOL CHAMPIONSHIPS

The ninth annual WIOL State Championships were held Saturday, February 23 at Bridle Trails State Park in Bellevue. The meet was hosted this year by the Sammamish Orienteers. The weather was warm and sunny (near 60). About 65 contestants from 15 high schools participated.

Winner of the men's division was Eric Bone, of Garfield (and COC). Rachel Tharp, of Timberline (and Nisqually Orienteers) captured the women's title.
The team championship was won by Bremerton.

Page 2

COMING MEETS

Farrell/McWhirter Park
Saturday, March 23

Farrel/McWhirter is the site of one of our Trim Courses, in cooperation with the Redmond Parks and Recreation Department. This Park is at its best in spring. Although mostly runnable, 'white' forest, Farrell/McWhirter collects nettles, berries and associated brush vegetation in the summer.
To get to Farrel/ McWhirter park, take SR 520 across the Evergreen Point Bridge to where it ends and becomes Avondale Road just east of Redmond. From here continue on Avondale Road about one mile, turn right on Novelty Hill Road, then left onto Redmond Road after about 1/2 mile. Park is on your left.

[Image: Site map, Farrel/McWhirter]

Lynndale Park
Sunday, April 7

Lynndale Park is a small, interesting park north of Edmonds with lots of green and deep ravines. The first orienteering task is finding the Park. Located away from main roads, and with minimal signing, getting there is half the fun. To reach the Park, take SW 196th St (SR 524) Exit from I-5 at Lynnwood. Go west through Lynnwood, and across SR 99 to 68th Ave. W. Then north to SW 189th St, and left to the main parking area of the Park. Since the developed area of the Park is heavily used for baseball and soccer in the spring, starts may be located around the perimeter. Follow signs to the designated start area.

Volunteer Park
Saturday, May 4

Replaces Lincoln Park

Join us for May orienteering as we return to Volunteer Park after a lapse of several years. This Park is more challenging than it might seem at first, with opportunity for open running, nooks and crannies you may normally never see, and lots and lots of spring flowers. Volunteer Park is located on Capitol Hill in Seattle.

Kittitas Valley Orienteering Festival
May 11/12
Note date change

If you missed the World Cup last August, here is your opportunity to check out the new Ros-Elum Ridge map. There will be a two day "B" meet the second weekend in May. With lots of terrain and lots of green (all shades), Ros-Elum is a challenging map both for runners and navigators. We are planning on at least five courses each day, with a limited number of preprinted courses. Further details in the next newsletter, but make your plans now.

The meet will be a joint effort of Ellensburg Orienteers and Cascade Orienteering. We need volunteers to help with starts and finishes and help with picking up markers both days. If you can help either day, please call Denise Klein at 322-7797.

MONTHLY MEETING

Monday, March 4, 7:00 pm

Guest speaker will be Susan Infield, UW psychology O'researcher. Susan is the one who has been doing all the interviews of orienteers. Come and find out if all they say about orienteers really is true. We have to be out of the library by nine, but rumor has it that there may be refreshments later.

ORIENTEERING VIDEOS

At the urging of Ardis Dull, the Seattle Public Library is acquiring orienteering videos. They now own Orienteering, All Welcome, a 12 minute upbeat introduction to orienteering, and Orienteering from the Classroom to the Forest, Winnie Stott's 85 minute tape directed towards teachers. These can be requested from the downtown library, or sent to your branch library.

BOARD BRIEFS

January 15, 1991
The Board met at Katie Sprugels. There was substantial discussion about hosting the USOF Convention, and how the convention and A meet could be coordinated and profits (if any) shared. Denise Klein and Peter Golde were to meet with Dave Enger and Dave Tallent. Bruce was asked to write a letter to WSOA expressing Cascade concerns.

February 12, 1991
The Board met at Bruce McAlister's following a meeting of the ad hoc USOF Convention meeting. All bills are paid, and we have about $1,700 in the bank. We have meet directors and course directors for coming meets except Lynndale. Authorized witing to USOF with a formal offer to host 1992 USOF Convention. Next meetings March 12 at Ardis Dull's, and April 9 at Denise Klein/David Soper's. Board meetings are always open to all members.

Page 3

CASCADE CHATTER

Compiled by Harvey Friedman

[Editorial content by Harvey Friedman regarding club roster changes, volunteering at meets, master map copying etiquette, and news about Rick Hood.]

FOR THE RECORD, of August 1990 - By Harvey Friedman

I finally have made time to present facts about our big international meet last summer. I was not entirely pleased with the tone of the coverage in "Orienteering North America" (ONA), the major O' magazine for this continent. There were no interviews with folks like Steve Templeton (a well-traveled orienteer and experienced mapping field-checker), who sought out the organizers of PNWOF to say that his course was the most challenging and enjoyable that he had done in the past few years; many others wanted to know when would be the next meet out west. Several WC#5 runners who stayed for the 2nd day of PNWOF were glad that Ros*Elum was used because the original Cle Elum Ridge area was too easy for them. After the numbers breakdown, I'll include quotes that others wrote about the event.

The final figures show that, including World Cup competitors, 805 individuals started at PNWOF. 817 registered and 764 participated on Saturday and 738 registered and 644 participated on Sunday (though several of a Swiss tour group picked up their maps but didn't run because they did not want to be late for their flight home). Reporting by gender, we had 461 PNWOF + 63 World Cup males and 237 PNWOF + 44 World Cup females for totals of 524 males and 281 females or a grand total of 805 different people participating. We also had about 60 or so people each day in the Direct Classes.

[For APOC90, we had the pre-registered IOF classes, some vacant slots in each class for the late-comers (all the IOF classes had pre-marked maps) and Direct Classes for people who wanted to go in groups or who could not get into an IOF class (because all the vacancies had already filled). These Direct Classes meant that people could sign up directly on the day they wanted to participate and copy their course from a master map. We offered beginner, advanced beginner, intermediate, and advanced courses which were roughly equivalent to the color courses white, yellow, orange, and red. Thus, families could go together if they wanted to, or that elite fellow from Sweden who didn't find about the meet until the previous week-end could still have a decent workout on a 'red' course. Rather than the pretentious and misleading 'competitive/recreational', we used 'pre-registered individual'/'Direct Class' for our dichotomies.]

Having 15 courses each day with 805 competitors total plus another 60 or so in the Direct Classes each day (and not the same 60 each day) should certainly qualify PNWOF90 as one of the biggest US meets ever. Bodies counts at scout meets where as many as a dozen kids run together hardly counts unless you are trying to sell O' to scouts.

It would be nice to say that this was the biggest North American meet in 1990 or in US history but the former is false and I am not sure about the latter. Each of the other individual days of APOC90 held in Canada drew more than the 805 that we had, despite rumors to the contrary we had the largest attendance of any meet held in the US in 1990. Since only 140 of the 805 attended from the US, we might have had the meet in the US withh)000*e largest number of participants from outside of North America EVER. Treating the APOC90 7-day event as a single meet (which it was because it had a single registration center and the same classes and course breakdown) and counting individuals who attended any of the 7 days yields 1320 individuals. I believe that 1320 plus all the Direct Class people at any of the 7 days makes the APOC 7-day the biggest meet ever held in North America.

QUOTABLES

From Per Stensby in his IOF delegate report:
... As the World Cup was given to the Washington State Orienteering Association, it was known that the clubs in the region had earlier organized only a few national events with no more than 100-200 competitors. Earlier visits by the IOF delegate to the competition area revealed exceptionally thick bands of vegetation, big ravines and a plenitude of (continuously changing) motorbike trails. In spite of these unfavorable factors, WSOA was able to organize a World Cup with passing grades.

From David Hogg, editor of the 'Australian Orienteer':
... Despite the shortcomings in the organisation, the orienteers still enjoyed their two days of competition and the hospitality offered by the local orienteers.

From Bill Wildprett, Silva Orienteering Services, USA:
... From what I have heard, your club put on a well run event. There are always detractors, but then they will be around, no matter what you do right. Don't worry about them.

From Carol McNeill, Great Britain team coordinator:
... On behalf of all the British Team our thanks for all the work you put into organising the World Cup.

From Peter Kullich, Lincolnshire, England:
... Are there any other events in the area this summer as we thoroughly enjoyed APOC - a great 'O' holiday.

From Julie De Pass, Silva compass & gear distributor for Canada:
... We both have great memories of your event, and I would like to take this opportunity to send you, Harvey, and all your valiant workers a BIG THANK YOU for all your superb efforts.
...Anyhow it was all a lot of fun and we have super memories of Cle Elum to look back on for many years to come. And somehow ith)000*de the trip across the continent really worthwhile......I certainly got my moneysworth!!!
... I just loved the terrain ... it was so different for us.

These excerpts and the many oral expressions of gratitude from many O'ers at the meet indicated to me that though PNWOF90 was not a perfect meet (whatever that may be), it was much better received than a small number have portrayed it, and almost all in attendance appreciated our friendship and hospitality. With this in mind, and knowing how to smooth out the rough edges of summer 1990, we should be able to handle our next large meet more easily. Again, I want to thank all who helped make WC#5 and PNWOF90 the successful weekend that was.

Page 4

MEET RESULTS

BOG SLOG
December 30
It had been a cold and wintry week. With up to 8 inches of snow before Christmas, the forecast was for another snowstorm on Sunday the 30th. Bellingham got another 8 to 12 inches of snow, but at St Edwards, there was intermittent light snow, with light winds. Not enough snow for a ski-O, but, with the cold and frozen ground, footing was good, and times were fast. But the pool was warm, and the party at Debbie's afterward was festive and well attended. Bruce McAlister was Meet Director and Don Atkinson was the Course Designer. Awards were made at the party. First places in Advanced were Peter Zurcher and Debbie Newell with Ben Hall and Pat Albright Zurcher second (men and women). Carl Coryell-Martin and Eileen Breseman were the pie winners on Course 2 Novice winner was Ian Taylor. Winners took home the usual reward of home-baked pies.

NAD PARK
February 2
A NISQUALLY ORIENTEERS MEET

It was a good thing that NAD Park was a small park, for this meet took place in a torrential downpour. On the drive back to the ferry, there were drowned cars in every dip. Undaunted by the weather Ben Hall and Anne York finished one, two in the intermediate course and Debbie Newell and Eileen Breseman beat out all the male competitors on the long (25 controls) course.

UW RELAY MEET
February 10

The only scheduled relay meet of the season was held on the UW campus. The meet was a benefit to raise funds to help send a UW team to the Intercollegiate meet at the end of April. The meet raised almost $200; together with some donations it appears there will be a 3-man team from the Northwest back in Michigan.
This was also a WIOL meet, with only five non-WIOL teams. The weather was warm and sunny, and a wild scene with runners approaching from all directions.

[Image: Eileen and Ryan Breseman at the U W Relays]

U W Relay
February 10, 1991
Meet Directors/Course Design: Jeff Coker, Nathan Tharp
Registration: Mike Tharp, Ardis Dull

  4. Judy Sozeloff, Cathy Smith, Bill Owen - 76:38
  5. Keith Johnson, Margie Bone

SEWARD PARK
February 16

The weather was cool and damp, but the rain had stopped before the meet began, and there were even a few rays of sunshine. With 55 WIOL runners and 63 regular meet runners, there were 118 orienteers out on the courses.
There were a lot of new novice runners. Jessica Rykken was first on the intermediate course with Steve Layman edging out Bruce McAlister by 13 seconds for second. On Course 3, it was Ben Hall 18 seconds ahead of Dave Enger.

COC Questionnaire

Dear COC Member,
It has been a few years since the club has sought direct feedback from the membership in the form of a questionnaire which allows for a useful exchange of information...

I. Your Orienteering Participation and Objectives

a. "I have been orienteering for:" (under 1 year) (1 to 4 years) (over 4 years). b. "Of my outdoor activities, Orienteering is" (my top activity) (one of my top 4 activities) (not one of my top 4 activities).
c. "Out of the total local events listed in the newsletter that are within an one hour's drive, in an average year I typically attend:" (0 to 3 events per year) (3 to 6 events) (6 to 12 events) (over 12 events per year). d. "In an average year, in addition to the events that I have indicated above (question c), I generally drive to _____ meets more than an hour's from here in Washington and _____ meets in Canada and fly to _____ meets.
e. "Of the events that COC has all or some responsibility for, in a typical year, I generally help out:" (never) (1 or 2 events per year) (3 to 6 events) (more than 6 events) f. "In my age class, my orienteering approach is primarily:" (recreational or wayfaring) (low key competitive) (mostly competitive) (very competitive).
g. "Over the next two years, I plan to do the following regarding the questions I"b" through I"f":" regarding b: (make it less important) (mostly maintain) (make it more important)
regarding c: (attend less local events) (mostly maintain) (attend more local events) regarding d: (reduce other days) (mostly maintain) (increase other days)
regarding e: (reduce frequency of help) (mostly maintain) (increase frequency of help) regarding f: (be less competitive) (mostly maintain) (be more competitive)
* h. "In brief, I participate in the sport of orienteering primarily because __________________________ and secondarily because __________________________; conversely what bothers me most about the sport is __________________________ and secondarily is __________________________."

II. COC's Offerings, Operations, and Orientation

a. Local meets requiring less than an hour's drive: (less often) (about the same) (more often) than we typically do. b. Distant "C" meets that require at least an hour's drive, such as a local meet at Cle Elum or Fort Casey: (less often) (about the same) (more oven) than we typically do.
c. Training Events and clinics: (less often) (about the same) (more often) than we do. d. Special O Events like Canoe, Ski, Night, Bike: (less often) (about the same) (more often) than we do.
e. "B" Events, like the state championship: (less often) (about the same) (more often). f. "A" Events, like the Pig Wars and other major meets: (less often) (about the same) (more often).
g. "Super A" Events (e.g.: US Championships, US Long "O" Championships, Conventions ) than are aimed to draw a lot of orienteers beyond the Pacific Northwest and Western Canada area and result in participants in excess of 200: (less often) (about the same) (more often). h. Updating local maps: (less than now) (about like now) (more than now).
i. Mapping new areas: (less than now) (about like now) (more than now). j. Club meetings and social events: (less often) (about the same) (more often).
k. Newsletter and other mailings: (less often) (about the same) (more often). l. Promotional and media coverage or support: (less so) (about the same) (more so)
m. Dues or meet fees: (decrease) (keep about the same) (increase). n. Development of simple "how to do sheets" and other ways of bringing newer members into positions of involvement. (less so) (about the same) (more so).
o. New members packet and support: (less than now) (about the same) (more than now) p. A phone call "I will attend" system for "C" meets that are more than an hour from here (disagree) (indifferent towards) (support).
q. String "O" and other youth and family oriented activities: (less than now) (about the same) (more than now). r. Scholastic orienteering league/events:(less than now)(about the same) (more than now)

"Of items II "a" through "r", I believe that for the next two or so year the club should focus on the following four: (1st) (2nd) (3rd) (4th)"
"Of items II "a" through "r", I believe that for the next two or so years the club should be very cautious regarding. (1st) (2nd) (3rd) (4th)"

III. Puget Sound and Pacific Northwest Orienteering club communication, coordination, or consultation.

a. Meet scheduling: (stand alone) (communicate with other clubs) (coordinate with other clubs) (consolidate efforts). b. "C" meets: (stand alone) (communicate ...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...).
c. "B" or "A" meets: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). d. Newsletters/result sheets: (stand alone...) (communicate...) (coordinate..) (consolidate..).
e. Training events: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). f. Mapping projects: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...).
g. Publicity and Media activities: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). h. Monies/treasury (stand alone) (consolidated..)
i. Orienteering Hot Line: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). j. Social events: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...).

IV. Member Support of COC

a. "I (believe) (am indifferent to) (do not believe) that the club should consider a two tier membership, with lower annual dues for those that agreed to help with some local meets for the year." b. "I (believe) (am indifferent to) (do not believe) that the club should adopt a two tier membership system, with a lower meet fee for those that have agreed to help with some local meets for the year."
c. "I (believe) (am indifferent to) (do not believe) that a club member should attend at least several local meets a year." d. "I (believe) (do not believe) that a club member should help with local meets at least several times a year."
e. "I (believe) (am indifferent to) (do not believe) that a club member should be involved in club committees or leadership positions or offer some extended service at least one year in four." f. "I (believe) (am indifferent to) (do not believe) that non-club members should be charged maybe double what club members are charged at meets."
g. "I believe that a better approach to having adequate help with club events might be....." h. "I (would) (would not) be willing to fill out a resource sheet so that the club would have a better idea of what I was willing to volunteer for, what I felt competent doing, and what I would like more exposure with. (Note: if you would please pick up one at a meet.)

V. Final Comments

What key questions important to you were not asked: Question 1, 2, 3. Please answer your own question(s): Answer 1, 2, 3.

(PLEASE OFFER ANY OTHER COMMENTS, CONCERNS, CRITICISMS, OR COUNSEL BELOW -- THANKS)

From: COC Member (Name optional)

block_index.text: Bearing 315 The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club | March - April, 1991

With a touch of spring in the air, we return to Lake Tradition Park. Lake Tradition lies on the first ridge to the east of Issaquah, and offers lots of contours and some marshes, ponds and a lake. Although there is very little open forest, most of the park is light green and runnable.

To reach the Park, take I-90 to the High Point Exit (Exit 20), several miles past Issaquah. Park along the exit road. Starts will be a short walk in from the parking area. Meet Director is Debbie Newell; courses by Glen Gordon. If you would like to help with registration/starts/finishes, please call Debbie at 488-7471. For latest meet information, call the Hotline: 783-3866.

COC is planning a 6 hr ROGAINE in the Tumac Mt area this August. Volunteer efforts gladly accepted. An 8 hr ROGAINE is planned near Tuscon, AZ on April 6, and RMOC is planning a 24 hr Colorado ROGAINE over Labor Day weekend. Bruce McAlister has details

> ATTENTION PLEASE > > Enclosed with this issue of Bearing 315 is a COC Questionnaire prepared by Rick Hood. A Club like ours depends upon knowing what YOU want. Please take the time to fill out and return the questionnaire - by mail (it takes 29c), or at any Club function. Family members please make copies and fill out individually. Extra copies (and stamps) available at club functions.
COC OFFER TO HOST '92 USOF CONVENTION

The COC Board has voted to notify USOF that COC will offer to host the 1992 USOF Convention. The proposed convention site is the UW Campus in early to mid July, after the 4th, but still well before the O-Ringen.

The Convention, typically Tuesday through Friday would be followed by a 2-day A meet. Early thinking has the A meet at Ros-Elum/Cle Elum Ridge. The convention would include various social and fun events; a canoe-o, night-o, and dinner at Blake Island. The Convention would be preceded by a Junior Training Camp.

Attendance at the Convention has typically been 75/100, with substantially more at the A meet. We want this to be a class-A event from the first welcoming to the final award presentation. This means a lot of work by a lot of people. All offers of help and ideas would be deeply appreciated. Committees are just being organized. Please call Bruce McAlister (821-6862), or leave a message on the Hot-Line if you are interested in helping.

The ninth annual WIOL State Championships were held Saturday, February 23 at Bridle Trails State Park in Bellevue. The meet was hosted this year by the Sammamish Orienteers. The weather was warm and sunny (near 60). About 65 contestants from 15 high schools participated.
Winner of the men's division was Eric Bone, of Garfield (and COC). Rachel Tharp, of Timberline (and Nisqually Orienteers) captured the women's title. The team championship was won by Bremerton.

Farrel/McWhirter is the site of one of our Trim Courses, in cooperation with the Redmond Parks and Recreation Department. This Park is at its best in spring. Although mostly runnable, 'white' forest, Farrell/McWhirter collects nettles, berries and associated brush vegetation in the summer. To get to Farrel/ McWhirter park, take SR 520 across the Evergreen Point Bridge to where it ends and becomes Avondale Road just east of Redmond. From here continue on Avondale Road about one mile, turn right on Novelty Hill Road, then left onto Redmond Road after about 1/2 mile. Park is on your left.

Lynndale Park is a small, interesting park north of Edmonds with lots of green and deep ravines. The first orienteering task is finding the Park. Located away from main roads, and with minimal signing, getting there is half the fun. To reach the Park, take SW 196th St (SR 524) Exit from I-5 at Lynnwood. Go west through Lynnwood, and across SR 99 to 68th Ave. W. Then north to SW 189th St, and left to the main parking area of the Park. Since the developed area of the Park is heavily used for baseball and soccer in the spring, starts may be located around the perimeter. Follow signs to the designated start area.

Join us for May orienteering as we return to Volunteer Park after a lapse of several years. This Park is more challenging than it might seem at first, with opportunity for open running, nooks and crannies you may normally never see, and lots and lots of spring flowers. Volunteer Park is located on Capitol Hill in Seattle.
Kittitas Valley Orienteering Festival May 11/12 Note date change

If you missed the World Cup last August, here is your opportunity to check out the new Ros-Elum Ridge map. There will be a two day "B" meet the second weekend in May. With lots of terrain and lots of green (all shades), Ros-Elum is a challenging map both for runners and navigators. We are planning on at least five courses each day, with a limited number of preprinted courses. Further details in the next newsletter, but make your plans now.

The meet will be a joint effort of Ellensburg Orienteers and Cascade Orienteering. We need volunteers to help with starts and finishes and help with picking up markers both days. If you can help either day, please call Denise Klein at 322-7797.

Guest speaker will be Susan Infield, UW psychology O'researcher. Susan is the one who has been doing all the interviews of orienteers. Come and find out if all they say about orienteers really is true. We have to be out of the library by nine, but rumor has it that there may be refreshments later.

At the urging of Ardis Dull, the Seattle Public Library is acquiring orienteering videos. They now own Orienteering, All Welcome, a 12 minute upbeat introduction to orienteering, and Orienteering from the Classroom to the Forest, Winnie Stott's 85 minute tape directed towards teachers. These can be requested from the downtown library, or sent to your branch library.

January 15, 1991 The Board met at Katie Sprugels. There was substantial discussion about hosting the USOF Convention, and how the convention and A meet could be coordinated and profits (if any) shared. Denise Klein and Peter Golde were to meet with Dave Enger and Dave Tallent. Bruce was asked to write a letter to WSOA expressing Cascade concerns.

February 12, 1991 The Board met at Bruce McAlister's following a meeting of the ad hoc USOF Convention meeting. All bills are paid, and we have about $1,700 in the bank. We have meet directors and course directors for coming meets except Lynndale. Authorized witing to USOF with a formal offer to host 1992 USOF Convention. Next meetings March 12 at Ardis Dull's, and April 9 at Denise Klein/David Soper's. Board meetings are always open to all members.
[Editorial content by Harvey Friedman regarding club roster changes, volunteering at meets, master map copying etiquette, and news about Rick Hood.]
FOR THE RECORD, of August 1990 - By Harvey Friedman

I finally have made time to present facts about our big international meet last summer. I was not entirely pleased with the tone of the coverage in "Orienteering North America" (ONA), the major O' magazine for this continent. There were no interviews with folks like Steve Templeton (a well-traveled orienteer and experienced mapping field-checker), who sought out the organizers of PNWOF to say that his course was the most challenging and enjoyable that he had done in the past few years; many others wanted to know when would be the next meet out west. Several WC#5 runners who stayed for the 2nd day of PNWOF were glad that RosElum was used because the original Cle Elum Ridge area was too easy for them. After the numbers breakdown, I'll include quotes that others wrote about the event.

The final figures show that, including World Cup competitors, 805 individuals started at PNWOF. 817 registered and 764 participated on Saturday and 738 registered and 644 participated on Sunday (though several of a Swiss tour group picked up their maps but didn't run because they did not want to be late for their flight home). Reporting by gender, we had 461 PNWOF + 63 World Cup males and 237 PNWOF + 44 World Cup females for totals of 524 males and 281 females or a grand total of 805 different people participating. We also had about 60 or so people each day in the Direct Classes.

[For APOC90, we had the pre-registered IOF classes, some vacant slots in each class for the late-comers (all the IOF classes had pre-marked maps) and Direct Classes for people who wanted to go in groups or who could not get into an IOF class (because all the vacancies had already filled). These Direct Classes meant that people could sign up directly on the day they wanted to participate and copy their course from a master map. We offered beginner, advanced beginner, intermediate, and advanced courses which were roughly equivalent to the color courses white, yellow, orange, and red. Thus, families could go together if they wanted to, or that elite fellow from Sweden who didn't find about the meet until the previous week-end could still have a decent workout on a 'red' course. Rather than the pretentious and misleading 'competitive/recreational', we used 'pre-registered individual'/'Direct Class' for our dichotomies.]

Having 15 courses each day with 805 competitors total plus another 60 or so in the Direct Classes each day (and not the same 60 each day) should certainly qualify PNWOF90 as one of the biggest US meets ever. Bodies counts at scout meets where as many as a dozen kids run together hardly counts unless you are trying to sell O' to scouts.

It would be nice to say that this was the biggest North American meet in 1990 or in US history but the former is false and I am not sure about the latter. Each of the other individual days of APOC90 held in Canada drew more than the 805 that we had, despite rumors to the contrary we had the largest attendance of any meet held in the US in 1990. Since only 140 of the 805 attended from the US, we might have had the meet in the US withh)000e largest number of participants from outside of North America EVER. Treating the APOC90 7-day event as a single meet (which it was because it had a single registration center and the same classes and course breakdown) and counting individuals who attended any of the 7 days yields 1320 individuals. I believe that 1320 plus all the Direct Class people at any of the 7 days makes the APOC 7-day the biggest meet ever held in North America.

From Per Stensby in his IOF delegate report: ... As the World Cup was given to the Washington State Orienteering Association, it was known that the clubs in the region had earlier organized only a few national events with no more than 100-200 competitors. Earlier visits by the IOF delegate to the competition area revealed exceptionally thick bands of vegetation, big ravines and a plenitude of (continuously changing) motorbike trails. In spite of these unfavorable factors, WSOA was able to organize a World Cup with passing grades.

From David Hogg, editor of the 'Australian Orienteer': ... Despite the shortcomings in the organisation, the orienteers still enjoyed their two days of competition and the hospitality offered by the local orienteers.

From Bill Wildprett, Silva Orienteering Services, USA: ... From what I have heard, your club put on a well run event. There are always detractors, but then they will be around, no matter what you do right. Don't worry about them.
From Carol McNeill, Great Britain team coordinator: ... On behalf of all the British Team our thanks for all the work you put into organising the World Cup.
From Peter Kullich, Lincolnshire, England: ... Are there any other events in the area this summer as we thoroughly enjoyed APOC - a great 'O' holiday.

From Julie De Pass, Silva compass & gear distributor for Canada: ... We both have great memories of your event, and I would like to take this opportunity to send you, Harvey, and all your valiant workers a BIG THANK YOU for all your superb efforts. ...Anyhow it was all a lot of fun and we have super memories of Cle Elum to look back on for many years to come. And somehow ith)000de the trip across the continent really worthwhile......I certainly got my moneysworth!!! ... I just loved the terrain ... it was so different for us.

These excerpts and the many oral expressions of gratitude from many O'ers at the meet indicated to me that though PNWOF90 was not a perfect meet (whatever that may be), it was much better received than a small number have portrayed it, and almost all in attendance appreciated our friendship and hospitality. With this in mind, and knowing how to smooth out the rough edges of summer 1990, we should be able to handle our next large meet more easily. Again, I want to thank all who helped make WC#5 and PNWOF90 the successful weekend that was.

BOG SLOG December 30 It had been a cold and wintry week. With up to 8 inches of snow before Christmas, the forecast was for another snowstorm on Sunday the 30th. Bellingham got another 8 to 12 inches of snow, but at St Edwards, there was intermittent light snow, with light winds. Not enough snow for a ski-O, but, with the cold and frozen ground, footing was good, and times were fast. But the pool was warm, and the party at Debbie's afterward was festive and well attended. Bruce McAlister was Meet Director and Don Atkinson was the Course Designer. Awards were made at the party. First places in Advanced were Peter Zurcher and Debbie Newell with Ben Hall and Pat Albright Zurcher second (men and women). Carl Coryell-Martin and Eileen Breseman were the pie winners on Course 2 Novice winner was Ian Taylor. Winners took home the usual reward of home-baked pies.
Meet Director: Bruce McAlister Course Design: Don Atkinson Registration Start: Sean Beasley Finish: Ardis Dull
Meet Director: Dave Petty Course Design: Don Atkinson
PT DEFIANCE PARK January 19 A NISQUALLY ORIENTEERS MEET
Meet Director: Miner Courses: Miner Registration: Miner Start: Miner Finish: Miner
NAD PARK February 2 A NISQUALLY ORIENTEERS MEET

It was a good thing that NAD Park was a small park, for this meet took place in a torrential downpour. On the drive back to the ferry, there were drowned cars in every dip. Undaunted by the weather Ben Hall and Anne York finished one, two in the intermediate course and Debbie Newell and Eileen Breseman beat out all the male competitors on the long (25 controls) course.
NAD Park Nisqually Orienteers February 2, 1991 Meet Director, Course Design: Bob Becker, Terry Bryant

The only scheduled relay meet of the season was held on the UW campus. The meet was a benefit to raise funds to help send a UW team to the Intercollegiate meet at the end of April. The meet raised almost $200; together with some donations it appears there will be a 3-man team from the Northwest back in Michigan. This was also a WIOL meet, with only five non-WIOL teams. The weather was warm and sunny, and a wild scene with runners approaching from all directions.
[Image: Eileen and Ryan Breseman at the U W Relays]
U W Relay February 10, 1991 Meet Directors/Course Design: Jeff Coker, Nathan Tharp Registration: Mike Tharp, Ardis Dull
Ben Hall, Rick Breseman, Jake Carton - 52:28
Debbie Newell, Dave Tallent, Mark Howlett - 55:00
Eileen Breseman, Jessica Rykken, Mike Schuh - 67:10
Judy Sozeloff, Cathy Smith, Bill Owen - 76:38

The weather was cool and damp, but the rain had stopped before the meet began, and there were even a few rays of sunshine. With 55 WIOL runners and 63 regular meet runners, there were 118 orienteers out on the courses. There were a lot of new novice runners. Jessica Rykken was first on the intermediate course with Steve Layman edging out Bruce McAlister by 13 seconds for second. On Course 3, it was Ben Hall 18 seconds ahead of Dave Enger.
Meet Director: Peter Golde Course Design: Ross Stevens Registration: John Nitardy Start: Roger Sharp Finish: Ross Stevens, Ardis Dull Others: Mike Schuh, Mike Tharp, Dave Tallent
Dear COC Member, It has been a few years since the club has sought direct feedback from the membership in the form of a questionnaire which allows for a useful exchange of information...
I. Your Orienteering Participation and Objectives

a. "I have been orienteering for:" (under 1 year) (1 to 4 years) (over 4 years). b. "Of my outdoor activities, Orienteering is" (my top activity) (one of my top 4 activities) (not one of my top 4 activities). c. "Out of the total local events listed in the newsletter that are within an one hour's drive, in an average year I typically attend:" (0 to 3 events per year) (3 to 6 events) (6 to 12 events) (over 12 events per year). d. "In an average year, in addition to the events that I have indicated above (question c), I generally drive to meets more than an hour's from here in Washington and meets in Canada and fly to meets. e. "Of the events that COC has all or some responsibility for, in a typical year, I generally help out:" (never) (1 or 2 events per year) (3 to 6 events) (more than 6 events) f. "In my age class, my orienteering approach is primarily:" (recreational or wayfaring) (low key competitive) (mostly competitive) (very competitive). g. "Over the next two years, I plan to do the following regarding the questions I"b" through I"f":" regarding b: (make it less important) (mostly maintain) (make it more important) regarding c: (attend less local events) (mostly maintain) (attend more local events) regarding d: (reduce other days) (mostly maintain) (increase other days) regarding e: (reduce frequency of help) (mostly maintain) (increase frequency of help) regarding f: (be less competitive) (mostly maintain) (be more competitive) h. "In brief, I participate in the sport of orienteering primarily because and secondarily because ; conversely what bothers me most about the sport is and secondarily is ."
II. COC's Offerings, Operations, and Orientation

a. Local meets requiring less than an hour's drive: (less often) (about the same) (more often) than we typically do. b. Distant "C" meets that require at least an hour's drive, such as a local meet at Cle Elum or Fort Casey: (less often) (about the same) (more oven) than we typically do. c. Training Events and clinics: (less often) (about the same) (more often) than we do. d. Special O Events like Canoe, Ski, Night, Bike: (less often) (about the same) (more often) than we do. e. "B" Events, like the state championship: (less often) (about the same) (more often). f. "A" Events, like the Pig Wars and other major meets: (less often) (about the same) (more often). g. "Super A" Events (e.g.: US Championships, US Long "O" Championships, Conventions ) than are aimed to draw a lot of orienteers beyond the Pacific Northwest and Western Canada area and result in participants in excess of 200: (less often) (about the same) (more often). h. Updating local maps: (less than now) (about like now) (more than now). i. Mapping new areas: (less than now) (about like now) (more than now). j. Club meetings and social events: (less often) (about the same) (more often). k. Newsletter and other mailings: (less often) (about the same) (more often). l. Promotional and media coverage or support: (less so) (about the same) (more so) m. Dues or meet fees: (decrease) (keep about the same) (increase). n. Development of simple "how to do sheets" and other ways of bringing newer members into positions of involvement. (less so) (about the same) (more so). o. New members packet and support: (less than now) (about the same) (more than now) p. A phone call "I will attend" system for "C" meets that are more than an hour from here (disagree) (indifferent towards) (support). q. String "O" and other youth and family oriented activities: (less than now) (about the same) (more than now). r. Scholastic orienteering league/events:(less than now)(about the same) (more than now)

"Of items II "a" through "r", I believe that for the next two or so year the club should focus on the following four: (1st) (2nd) (3rd) (4th)" "Of items II "a" through "r", I believe that for the next two or so years the club should be very cautious regarding. (1st) (2nd) (3rd) (4th)"
III. Puget Sound and Pacific Northwest Orienteering club communication, coordination, or consultation.

a. Meet scheduling: (stand alone) (communicate with other clubs) (coordinate with other clubs) (consolidate efforts). b. "C" meets: (stand alone) (communicate ...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). c. "B" or "A" meets: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). d. Newsletters/result sheets: (stand alone...) (communicate...) (coordinate..) (consolidate..). e. Training events: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). f. Mapping projects: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). g. Publicity and Media activities: (stand alone) (communicate...) (coordinate...) (consolidate...). h. Monies/treasury (stand alone) (consolidated..) i. a. "I (believe) (am indifferent to) (do not believe) that the club should consider a two tier membership, with lower annual dues for those that agreed to help with some local meets for the year." b. "I (believe) (am indifferent to) (do not believe) that the club should adopt a two tier membership system, with a lower meet fee for those that have agreed to help with some local meets for the year." c.
